Title: Nutrient depletion as a key factor for manipulating gene expression and product formation in different branches of the flavonoid pathway

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: UGT78D2 is needed for 3-O-glucosylation and stable accumulation of anthocyanins and contributes to flavonol glucosides; nutrient depletion induces its expression.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: heterologous expression; mutant analysis (ugt78d2); transcript profiling
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion; high light intensity; sucrose
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT78D2 glucosylates cyanidin, kaempferol and quercetin at the 3 position. 2. In ugt78d2 mutants, anthocyanin content drops to about 20% of WT and 3-glucosylated flavonols are reduced. 3. Its transcripts are induced by nutrient depletion and strongly by high light and sucrose.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent downstream engineering target to stabilize accumulated anthocyanins/flavonols after boosting biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Glycosylation is emphasized as a key determinant of differential flavonoid accumulation.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: UGT75C1 is responsible for 5-O-glycosylation in vivo and specifically affects cyanidin derivatives rather than flavonols.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT75C1 catalyzes anthocyanin 5-O-glycosylation in vivo. 2. ugt75c1 mutants alter cyanidin derivatives but not flavonols. 3. It is part of the anthocyanin derivatization module discussed in the review.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for tailoring anthocyanin chemical forms and stability rather than total pathway flux.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not highlighted as a major nutrient-responsive transcript compared with UGT78D2.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TT19 is essential for vacuolar accumulation of cyanins and is strongly induced by nitrogen or phosphorus deficiency.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis; co-expression analysis; transcript profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT19 is required for anthocyanin accumulation in the vacuole. 2. It is strongly induced under mineral nutrient deficiency. 3. Transport/sequestration capacity is therefore a major determinant of nutrient-induced anthocyanin accumulation.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important companion target for anthocyanin engineering to ensure vacuolar sequestration and visible accumulation.

Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: GL3; EGL3; TT8; PAP1; PAP2; TT2
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TTG1 is necessary for expression of late anthocyanin genes and for cyanin/procyanidin production, but is constitutively expressed and not nutrient responsive.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TTG1 forms part of the conserved MYB-bHLH-WD40 regulatory module. 2. It is necessary for expression of late genes such as DFR and ANS. 3. Its transcript level is not strongly changed by nutrient deprivation.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful enabling component, but not a nutrient-responsive switch; manipulation may cause epidermal trait pleiotropy.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ANL2 is important for cyanin accumulation in vegetative tissue, but its expression is not much influenced by mineral nutrient depletion.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genetic analysis cited; transcript profiling summary
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANL2 contributes to cyanin accumulation in vegetative tissue. 2. Its exact mode of action on flavonoid synthesis remains unclear. 3. It is not strongly nutrient responsive in the summarized datasets.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tissue-patterning factor for anthocyanin distribution, though mechanism remains unclear.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DHS1 is a shikimate-pathway control point potentially linking photosynthetic redox state to precursor supply for phenylalanine and flavonoids, but nutrient transcript responses are complex.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: functional characterization; heterologous expression with thioredoxin dependence; transcript profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DHS1 encodes the first enzyme of the shikimate pathway. 2. Heterologously expressed DHS1 requires reduced thioredoxin for activity, suggesting redox regulation linking photosynthesis to aromatic precursor supply. 3. The review notes complex and isoform-specific responses of DHS genes to nutrients.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Interesting upstream precursor-control target, but complexity and uncertainty of isoform roles limit direct deployment.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review highlights it as a crucial control point, though not a central flavonoid structural target.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: entry step from phenylalanine into phenylpropanoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: deaminates phenylalanine to cinnamic acid, committing primary metabolism to phenylpropanoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased PAL1 expression is associated with increased flavonoid/phenolic accumulation under nitrogen or phosphorus depletion, sucrose, high light, and PAP1 overexpression; loss of PAL1 together with PAL2 strongly reduces kaempferol glycosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; mutant analysis (pal1, pal2, pal1 pal2); PAL activity and metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion; sucrose; high light intensity; PAP1 overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: PAL1 is one of the two most strongly expressed PAL genes in Arabidopsis and is enhanced in tissues with high lignin synthesis and under flavonoid-inducing conditions
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: UV protection and stress-responsive phenolic accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAL1 transcript increased about threefold under nitrogen or phosphorus deficiency. 2. PAL1 also responded positively to sucrose, high light, and PAP1 overexpression. 3. Reduced PAL activity in pal mutants most strongly decreased kaempferol glycosides, supporting PAL1 contribution to flavonoid flux.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream flux-control gene for boosting phenylpropanoid-derived nutritional flavonoids, but branch-specific effects and pathway balancing should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix ATH1 microarray data summarized from Scheible et al. 2004; Misson et al. 2005; Morcuende et al. 2007; Vanderauwera et al. 2005; Solfanelli et al. 2006; Lloyd & Zakhleniuk 2004
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Different phenylpropanoid branches respond differently; lignin-related and flavonoid-related outputs may be differentially affected.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper is a review synthesizing evidence; PAL3 likely minor due to low expression and high Km, while PAL4 may be more lignin-related.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: activation of hydroxycinnamates to CoA esters
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts p-coumaric acid and related cinnamates into corresponding CoA thiol esters for downstream phenylpropanoid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: 4CL3 is the most nutrient-responsive 4CL gene and is associated with flavonoid synthesis rather than lignin synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: heterologous expression of 4CL family members; promoter::GUS tissue expression; microarray transcript profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion; sucrose; high light intensity; PAP1 overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Differentially expressed compared with 4CL1/4CL2; 4CL3 promoter active outside vascular tissue
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: production of protective flavonoids under stress/light
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 4CL3 was the most responsive 4CL gene to nutrient depletion across studies. 2. It is induced together with the anthocyanin regulon by PAP1 overexpression, nutrient deficiency, high light and sucrose. 3. Its expression pattern suggests a stronger role in flavonoid than lignin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising precursor-activation gene to enhance flavonoid accumulation in nonvascular tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point not firmly established
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix ATH1 microarray summaries from multiple published datasets
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could influence allocation between lignin and flavonoid branches depending on tissue context.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: At1g20490 is also discussed as a candidate 4CL linked to anthocyanin regulon, but 4CL3 is emphasized as the core nutrient-responsive enzyme.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: first committed step of flavonoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: condenses one p-coumaroyl-CoA with three malonyl-CoA molecules to produce naringenin chalcone
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHS expression is stimulated by nutrient depletion, high light and sucrose, supporting elevated flavonoid production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; literature synthesis from multiple species
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion; high light intensity; sugars; UV and blue light
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS is the first committed enzyme for all flavonoids. 2. CHS transcripts increased close to twofold under nitrogen depletion and two- to threefold under phosphorus depletion in Arabidopsis. 3. CHS also responds positively to high light and sucrose.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for broad enhancement of total flavonoid production under controlled nutrient/light regimes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad activation may alter multiple flavonoid branches rather than a single desired product.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: central hydroxylation step from flavanones to dihydroflavonols
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor for flavonols and anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: F3H is positively induced by nitrogen and phosphorus depletion, supporting increased flux toward both anthocyanins and flavonols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; cited mutant evidence showing FLS cannot replace F3H in vivo
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3H expression is enhanced in Arabidopsis under nitrogen or phosphorus depletion. 2. It provides the central dihydroflavonol intermediate required for both flavonol and anthocyanin branches. 3. In vivo, F3H is essential because FLS cannot substitute in f3h knockout plants.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful shared-branch engineering target to elevate both anthocyanin and flavonol pools when combined with downstream branch controls.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: adds a 3'-hydroxyl group to flavonoids, directing formation of cyanidin- and quercetin-type products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: F3'H expression increases under nitrogen and phosphorus depletion, promoting synthesis of cyanidin and quercetin branch products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; protein interaction evidence cited
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3'H is positively regulated by nitrogen and phosphorus depletion in Arabidopsis. 2. It is important for anthocyanin colour and quercetin/cyanidin branch formation. 3. It interacts with CHI, CHS and DFR, suggesting metabolon organization for anthocyanin synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong branch-directing target to shift flavonoid composition toward quercetin/cyanidin derivatives.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts dihydroflavonols to flavonols by introducing the C2-C3 double bond
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: FLS1 is strongly induced by high light and sucrose but only weakly by nitrogen or phosphorus depletion; nonetheless flavonol accumulation under nutrient stress suggests additional regulation beyond FLS1 transcript abundance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; knockout evidence; regulatory genetics with MYB12/MYB11/MYB111
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: high light intensity; sucrose; nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FLS1 is a key flavonol branch gene strongly responsive to high light and sucrose. 2. It is not part of the PAP1/PAP2 regulon and is little affected by nutrient depletion. 3. Nutrient depletion still increases flavonols, implying added control through other FLSs, glycosylation, or stability mechanisms.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important target for specific enhancement of flavonols, especially under light/sucrose-promoting contexts rather than nutrient depletion alone.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: FLS has some in vitro F3H activity, but does not compensate for F3H loss in vivo.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: reduces dihydroflavonols to leucoanthocyanidins using NADPH
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DFR is strongly induced by nutrient deficiency, supporting major anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; transactivation assays with PAP1/PAP2 plus GL3/EGL3 on DFR promoter
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR is a late anthocyanin gene strongly induced under nitrogen and phosphorus deficiency, often 15-20 fold. 2. Its promoter is activated by PAP1/PAP2 together with GL3/EGL3. 3. DFR contributes to cyanidin-type anthocyanin production in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for boosting anthocyanin content, especially in combination with upstream regulatory factors.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts leucoanthocyanidins to anthocyanidins in the anthocyanin pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms direct precursor of stable anthocyanin glycosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ANS is strongly induced by nitrogen and phosphorus depletion and supports anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: microarray transcript profiling; literature-based in vitro enzyme assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANS is among the strongly nutrient-induced late anthocyanin genes. 2. Increased ANS expression parallels anthocyanin accumulation under nutrient stress. 3. ANS may also have additional in vitro activity related to quercetin formation, though in vivo relevance remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful late-pathway target for raising anthocyanin accumulation but may require glycosylation/sequestration support.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Possible in vivo contribution to differentiated kaempferol/quercetin accumulation is speculative.

Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL1; 4CL3; anthocyanin pathway genes including glycosyltransferases, acyltransferases and GSTs
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: globally activates structural genes of the flavonoid pathway
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: major activator integrating environmental signals to amplify flavonoid synthesis
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with TTG1 and with bHLH partners GL3/EGL3
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: nitrogen deficiency; phosphorus deficiency; high light intensity; sucrose
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced under stress-related and carbon/light-promoting conditions; important in leaves and seedlings
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAP1 overexpression upregulates about 20 structural flavonoid genes and enhances anthocyanin/flavonoid synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion; high light intensity; sucrose
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP1 globally enhances expression of many flavonoid structural genes. 2. Most genes induced in PAP1 overexpressors are also induced by nutrient depletion, high light and sucrose. 3. PAP1 acts as a convergence point for multiple abiotic signals controlling anthocyanin production.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong master-regulator candidate to elevate broad flavonoid content, though broad activation may affect multiple branches and traits.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix ATH1 microarray summaries from pap1D and treatment datasets

Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: anthocyanin pathway structural genes overlapping PAP1 regulon
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates flavonoid structural genes similarly to PAP1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: especially strongly induced by nutrient depletion and likely central to nutrient deficiency signaling into the flavonoid pathway
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with TTG1 and bHLH partners GL3/EGL3
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: nitrogen deficiency; phosphorus deficiency; high light intensity; sucrose
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low under non-stress conditions but strongly induced under nitrogen deficiency, where transcript abundance can exceed PAP1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAP2 is a major nutrient-responsive activator promoting anthocyanin/flavonoid gene expression during nitrogen and phosphorus deficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-PCR; microarray transcript profiling; comparative expression analyses
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: nitrogen depletion; phosphorus depletion; high light intensity; sucrose
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP2 is strongly induced by nitrogen and phosphorus deficiency. 2. Under nitrogen deficiency, PAP2 transcript abundance can surpass PAP1. 3. PAP2 likely mediates nutrient stress activation of structural flavonoid genes.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Particularly attractive regulator for engineering nutrient-stress-inducible anthocyanin enhancement.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix ATH1 microarray summaries; RT-PCR data cited

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates flavonol-pathway and early flavonoid genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: specific regulator of the flavonol branch; essential together with MYB11/MYB111 for flavonol synthesis
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Mainly expressed in roots; can be expressed in rosette leaves
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MYB12 positively correlates with flavonol accumulation and activates FLS1 and other early genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: promoter/GUS assays; triple-mutant analysis; expression-metabolite correlation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB12 is a dedicated flavonol regulator. 2. It activates FLS1 and also CHS, CHI and F3H. 3. Together with MYB11 and MYB111 it is essential for flavonol synthesis, whereas anthocyanin accumulation can still occur without them.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for selective enhancement of nutritional flavonols without broadly activating anthocyanin branch.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray evidence and promoter-reporter studies cited

Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: essential component of the MYB11/12/111 flavonol regulatory module
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Mainly expressed in leaves/cotyledons; can be similar to MYB12 in rosette leaves
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MYB111 promotes flavonol synthesis through activation of FLS1 and other early genes.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB111 activates FLS1 and early flavonoid genes. 2. It is preferentially expressed in cotyledons/leaves. 3. Along with MYB12 and MYB11 it is required for flavonol accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suitable for tissue-targeted flavonol biofortification in leaf tissues.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray evidence and promoter-reporter studies cited

Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: DFR promoter and late anthocyanin genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: co-activates DFR and late anthocyanin gene expression with PAP1/PAP2
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: required for nitrogen-depletion-induced anthocyanin accumulation in cotyledons and rosette leaves
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with TTG1 and PAP1/PAP2 in MYB-bHLH-WD40 regulatory complex
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: GL3 transcripts are strongly induced, about sixfold, in rosette leaves by nitrogen depletion
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GL3 is essential for anthocyanin induction by nitrogen depletion in leaves; gl3 mutants fail to accumulate anthocyanins under N depletion.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis (gl3, egl3); transcript analysis; DFR promoter transactivation assays cited
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GL3 expression is strongly induced by nitrogen depletion. 2. gl3 mutants lack anthocyanin induction under nitrogen depletion in leaves, whereas egl3 mutants respond normally. 3. GL3 acts with PAP MYBs to activate late anthocyanin genes such as DFR.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important co-regulator for nutrient-responsive anthocyanin engineering, particularly in leaf tissues.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: GL3 also participates in epidermal trait regulation, so pleiotropic effects are possible.
